sentences.text: Yes.
That was in Kolki, that where the ghetto was.
And I didn't mention about what was more-- what was done in the time from the when we was in the ghetto.
The Germans, they take-- they tell-- they make Jewish police in the ghetto.
They called them Judenrat.
And they tell them every day, every week new things.
It's enough what we don't have what to eat, we don't have clothes.
They tell them, the Judenrat, if you will not bring us gold, silver, all this stuff, we will kill you tomorrow.
After tomorrow is the people from the Judenrat.
No, there's no way to compare, they was like the Jewish police.
They come to each home and ask for some-- you have gold.
You have something.
Give it.
Because they said that the Germans will begin the pogrom.
They will kill us.
Who-- everybody who got something-- gold things, every time give up and give up.
That way they rob us at all.
Nobody [INAUDIBLE] good thing.
Now I will tell you that's when we come out from the warehouse.
The Germans came and the police, the Ukraine police.
The Ukraine police-- the Ukraines, it was a lot of them in the police.
It was like big, big helpers that's no bandits was they was robbed the Jewish people.
They killed the same-- the same like the Germans.
Later, I will tell you-- and I will show you laters once we got from Russia what the Ukraine was.
They said what was happened to some once what no-- I want to go further.
I will-- I will end my sentence with that.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was the-- when we come out, they have horses and wagons.
And they say to us, you will go with us.
We will show what to do.
We didn't understand what happened then.
Then they come with the horses and with the wagons, we come to the place, to the ghetto.
We see it's quiet.
Nobody is in ghetto.
We understand what happened at that.
They take-- they tell us to take all the stuff, what we will find in the houses.
Everything, everything put in the wagons and take to the warehouse.
When we came in the ghetto, I see it's-- the killed people in the street, everywhere.
I and my father, we came to the-- [SOB] to the-- excuse me, please.
We came-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] we came to the house where we was living.
And I came in they called the mother, the sister.
Nobody answer.
I went-- and the father-- to the next houses.
Nobody is in the houses.
And the Germans, the police came after us and begin to beat us.
Work faster.
Take-- take everything.
I want to tell-- when we came in the ghetto, somebody-- we saw what somebody was taking everything from the houses.
Because everything in the houses, it already was mixed, everything.
But somebody was coming.
Maybe they-- they checked each house they would take us.
As we begin, we take the killed people and we put them on the wagons.
And we take them-- they show us where to take the-- the killed people-- killed people.
And we take it.
And we saw this place, it's terrible.
You can-- it's-- it's impossible to-- to explain.
[SOB] [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS]
We finished with this, all the job.
We take everything.
Know what to do.
They tell us we will not touch.
You will stay-- you will [INAUDIBLE].
No.
You understand what after the pogrom what it was was a kill.
It's left.
No.
Maybe, but no.
With these people, what we work for-- for them.
It was maybe 60 people or 70 people, I don't remember exactly.
Then after that, it's come people a little more.
They said they make, you know, papers, or how to call flyers.
But who was not killed, he's in life.
We will not touch more.
You-- you will leave, no?
The problem is was we begin to leave.
In a time, maybe like five days, six days, it's come people more.
The families-- it was broken families.
From some families came-- for example, came the husband, the wife, and the children and is dead.
Some ones came children.
The parents is dead.
No, it's a situation where the people-- everyone have a problem.
And it's-- it's-- it's the worst was it can be for a-- for a-- know, for a man to-- to-- to-- to have a thing-- to have like that.
You cannot sleep.
You can eat.
You cannot-- you know, and they asked us to work and to work and to work and a bit to work.
We stay you know.
All the people, what they came back to the ghetto, maybe 300 people, 400 people.
And that time, what was the pogrom, it was no partisans.
No partisans.
Partisans I will mention-- I'll tell you a little later.
And the Ukraine, what I-- they was doing-- they were done very much bad for who was no [INAUDIBLE] from the killing.
Nobody give food.
And a lot of them, the Ukraine that take-- take the people and brought to the Germans back, it's-- you cannot explain [INAUDIBLE], but what they was done.
And we begin again to work.
And each day, maybe we were-- were staying maybe three weeks or four weeks, I don't remember exactly.
One night-- oh, I didn't mention in that time, it was from Poland family.
That was two brothers.
Age, maybe they was a little older than me, maybe for a year or two years older.
Two brothers and a sister.
There was in two.
They jumped somewhere.
They came back into the ghetto.
They tell us-- one day, they tell me, the brother, what-- something will happen again.
They were-- they was repairing gold stuff.
They repair watches.
They repair some rings for the Germans.
And its was always two Germans in-- in Kolki.
One German, it was, don't know the last names there.
One is [INAUDIBLE] and the other was the name by [?
Bartz. ?] I remember the names.
And maybe more I've forgotten their names, I will remember who long I will live.
And they tell us-- the two brothers-- what [?
Bartz ?] and [INAUDIBLE] comes in the evening and he take all the stuff they was working, the gold stuff.
And they-- they take.
That mean something will happen it again.
I told my father what, you know, and that's-- and that's what-- what It's something can happen.
We-- we must be-- to do something.
You know, what we decide not to come to sleep in the ghetto.
We left to sleep where we work in the-- in the-- in the-- in the farm.
Where it's a farm, it was a big barn.
And one night, we didn't come.
And we stay in the barn.
We went.
It was straw and [INAUDIBLE].
And the father and a little more people would come.
And we stayed in morning.
It's come up the sun.
And we hear what people, Ukraine people comes and feed the-- the animals, the horses, the-- the cows.
You know, and I see we were saying to near to the father.
But I can hear him and he hears me.
Excuse me.
I say, father, we don't hear nothing.
Maybe-- maybe it's something wrong.
We decided to come out-- out.
When we come out from the straw and we saw they come.
It was on the roof, the top where the straw.
And they saw us and begin to-- to call [NON-ENGLISH], meaning in Anglo, Jews, come out.
Well, they take out us from the hole with a ladder, we come down.
It's a-- the Germans around.
The father begin to talk to them in German.
And to ask them what-- they beat the father.
They beat me.
They beat the rest of them.
No, we saw that the situation, it's terrible.
That's all.
Nothing to do.
They take us to the ghetto-- to the ghetto.
And it was, I remember, like now, it was a sunny day.
Very-- it was summertime or it goes to the fall time.
They take us to the ghetto.
And they put us in the ghetto.
And I saw that the brothers, too.
They was looking everywhere to find more the Jews who was left-- that's from our second pogrom.
The guy, the Jewish guy that's from Poland-- I don't remember his name.
He said, what I have a pistol.
You know, I don't know how [INAUDIBLE] because didn't check how.
We will go, young people.
We will go first.
And maybe in the way, what they will take us.
We will try to-- to something to come out.
When I-- in the-- in the ghetto, in the house, I tell my father.
And I tell him that's-- and that's what the guys, they have a pistol.
Maybe come with us together.
We will-- we will try what is-- anyway, we will be killed.
The time in the house I cannot explain to you.
It's not enough words what the people was feeling.
It's crying.
It's who cry, who-- who-- who, no-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Terrible feeling.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When they brought all the people, it's become maybe 2 o'clock day time.
They make no corridor from the house, a corridor from both sides, policemen and Germans.
And they catch the people and bring them to the door, go out, and they beat them.
The time in the house was-- everybody want to live maybe 5 minutes more, 10 minutes more, until the one truck will go and come back, or it will-- or they will come with the other truck.
They take everybody and they put them in with-- with the sticks.
They beat us.
And they put us in the truck.
Oh, I didn't say one thing.
When I told the father, father will go-- come with me and we'll go together.
He say, my son, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I don't-- [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] I don't want to see your-- you will killed.
And I want to see what I will-- well, you will see that I've been killed.
I decided I will go first.
And the father left the house.
[SOB] [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] They put in the trucks.
And we-- we sit in the trucks.
The foot must be straight.
And to each other straight we sit.
And on the feet, they put other people-- they fill up the truck.
But you cannot get up because somebody sit on your feet.
I got my place what I sit.
I was in the corner, the back.
I was in the back side from the truck.
And they left side.
It was in the corner from the left, how it's called-- the board from the truck.
You know, if they fill up, the couple Germans sit in the-- in the car.
And outside, maybe five policemen with-- with-- with guns.
No, and they begin-- they take us from the ghetto somewhere to the-- to the where it was-- the forest.
From that time, we know that they kill, the place.
We saw that's where we're going.
We decide where we see where they take us the same, in the same forest, there was killed our families in the first pogrom.
When they came-- no, we came out from the ghetto, from the city maybe.
This was maybe three, four, five kilometers.
And it was ground.
The truck begin go slowly.
The guy get up and begin to shot the in-- with his gun.
The gun didn't work.
When he do this, the police-- the police kill him in second.
They kill him on the truck.
Yes.
The guy what I say that the-- from-- the-- the Polish guy.
It was two brothers.
Jewish people.
Jewish people.
The one who had the gun.
They was together, yes.
We came together with them in the truck.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When I-- I know-- I don't know from where it come, the energy from where it come, but it's to make a decision like that.
I will tell you the true.
When we sat on the-- only in the truck-- and I was looking near to me the people.
Take the men and put them on the ground.
He will not go.
He's-- He's dead.
They didn't go.
Some people-- it was different people.
To older people, young people.
From where it was the energy in-- by myself, I don't know.
I jumped from the truck.
And I jumped through-- it was a fence.
One fence and the second fence.
And I jumped.
And to-- and it was like field, nothing-- no houses.
And I begin to run.
They-- the day was a sunny day.
When I jumped, they was killing the other people.
And the Ukraine-- it was people-- they begin to-- to-- to the policemen.
And they said, [NON-ENGLISH].
That mean that you see one Jew is running.
They-- the policeman comes to the-- to the corner, begin to shot after me.
What luck I got, I don't know.
He didn't shot me.
I come to a place that's all-- that's minutes and seconds.
It's not what you can-- you can something thinks what to do.
I come to a barn.
And the barn stay-- it's in the city.
It's in the side city.
And it was a pig in the small barn.
I think fast.
Open the door.
And I get up on the top.
In the top.
And it was straw.
When I come in the top, when no-- I feel what my-- my-- I'm shaking.
I-- I don't know what I'm doing, no?
It's something-- something slipped something.
My brain was working a little.
I put on myself the straw.
It was straw and they put in the corner.
I feel with my hand what enough.
It's a pain.
And I see it's blood in my nose.
And it's a lot in my hand-- blood.
I was think they shot me.
They wounded me.
No.
I feel my hand.
I feel my foot.
When I stayed at in this place and wait what it will be more.
It's best maybe, no, I'll tell you maybe three minutes, four minutes.
It's opened up the-- the barn.
It's come inside-- policemen.
And I hear what they shot-- shots more because the barn was near to the highway where the truck was standing in what we take.
The policeman comes with the owner from the-- I think what this was the owner.
And he said to him, [NON-ENGLISH].
That mean a Jew come in the barn.
No.
I don't know where-- if I got something inside, where it was, I don't know.
But I know he will come now.
He will shot me, or kill me, or what he will do.
And my luck, when he comes on the top, Understand that he take the straw from one side the other side.
He take from the middle and begin to pull them in one side and the other side.
And he put on me maybe a couple more straw.
And I hear what he said, where he-- where he can be?
He's not here.
Then, something's [BACKGROUND NOISE] easier on my heart become and he come down.
And then, I stay-- and it was maybe from the police where they shot all the people.
Maybe it was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] no, maybe a quarter mile, less than a quarter mile.
Very close.
I hear the shots with the shots.
I hear how the car-- the truck left with the people.
They shot the [INAUDIBLE].
They put them in the truck back.
Then they put-- they take the womans and the children.
And they don't put them on the truck.
They-- they-- no, how-- they tell them to walk to the-- to the places.
And I hear from the-- from the barn how they cry, how they-- it's terrible voices what how long I will live.
They-- [SOB] [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] I will never forget.
And in a couple hours, it become quiet.
They killed everybody, and it become quiet.
And it's come in maybe-- I don't know how you say.
What, in a couple hours come the owner from the barn and say to the survivor, who was a lady.
I don't-- I don't know who was that.
And he said what the policemen, they-- the Germans tell her to take the-- the-- where they killed the people.
Put them and to bring them to the-- to the places where they shot the-- all the Jewish people.
And I hear what he explained to the-- to the lady.
What they shot a lot of people and what they're doing.
They shot all of them.
And he left for a while.
It's hard to tell, a half hour or 40 minutes.
Then he come back.
And he said what he put the killed people.
He put them-- he take them to the place.
Know what to do.
I hear-- but I cannot come down.
What to do further?
I don't know what to do?
Then, I stayed that's the-- until night.
I need to wait to the night, nighttime what to do.
I decided I was beginning something.
My brain begin a little to work.
I was thinking-- I was born in the Komarove-- that's the village.
I know the people.
Where I know the-- the places where I can hide myself.
And I-- I-- I need to go, and how to go.
If I will come back to the ghetto, they have a bridge.
The bridge was-- stay all the time, was standing police and the Germans.
What nobody will blow up the bridge or what they was thinking.
I cannot go through-- through the bridge.
I decided to go in another way that's will-- that will take me more-- more time.
No, I think it will be more safer than to go to the bridge.
It's come-- no, maybe by middle of the night.
It was maybe-- maybe by 12 o'clock at night, I begin to go out.
And come to the door, I cannot open the door.
It's locked.
It was locked from the outside.
I cannot open the door.
What to do?
I come again on the top where the straw.
And if you know, they got covers-- the roofs with straw they make them.
I decided I will break the straw from the top to take and make a hole, and I will come down.
I take with me in the-- in-- in the barn, I take with me-- how it's-- that's-- pick.
[PAUSES FOR 17 SECONDS]
Pitchfork.
I forgot that.
And I come down.
And I begin to run.
They run.
I go across-- I went across the road, the highway, what the trucks went-- went.
And to the forest, I got maybe half mile to run.
Believe me, the-- a quarter mile, or I don't know exactly.
I was falling and get up, falling and get up.
And it's-- my face-- it's standing, the Germans, the police where they want to kill.
I feel what I'm not dead and not in life.
Until I run that's the-- and the time is going.
I come to the forest from the-- from the houses, what-- what I was the barn.
And I fall down.
And I rest a little.
And it's night.
I never was in a time like this in forest.
No, and I begin again to think how to go to the-- to my village, I will go.
I know the-- the way how to go.
I cannot go by the highways.
I begin to watch the highway at nighttime and decide to go.
I went maybe-- I got to go to-- to the my-- my-- my village.
It's maybe 12, 13 kilometers.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- at nighttime hiking to one place.
I see people sit around a fire.
And they begin to talk what is happening today in ghetto, in Kolki [INAUDIBLE].
Maybe it was four or five people.
What they was doing this time in the forest, I don't know.
You know, I-- when I heard what they talking, I feel that I cannot come to them.
And I cannot talk to them.
And I cannot ask them for help.
I go with a side in, and I begin to run.
Because I need to run that's at nighttime.
If I will not measure-- not make the time to come, at daytime I am afraid that somebody will kill me.
I came to the-- to a village, Kulikowicze.
I came to the village, Kulikowicze, from village Kulikowicze.
OK.
